title: Resources & Reading List
text:
If you’re new to LLM agents, start with these:

Lilian Weng — LLM Powered Autonomous Agents (June 2023)
The most widely cited blog post introduction to the field. Covers planning, memory, tool use with great diagrams.
ReAct paper (Yao et al., 2022)
The canonical agent loop: Reason + Act interleaved. Essential reading.
A Survey on Large Language Model based Autonomous Agents (Wang et al., 2023)
Comprehensive academic survey covering construction, application, and evaluation.

title: Resources & Reading List
text:
About This Survey
This survey was compiled in March 2026 by John P. Lake. It covers the LLM agent literature from 2022 through early 2026.
View source on GitHub · Report an issue

title: Memory, Tools & Actions
text: Three capabilities transform an LLM from a text generator into an agent: memory (persisting information beyond the context window), tools (calling external capabilities), and actions (operating in digital or physical environments). This section surveys the research landscape for each.

title: Memory, Tools & Actions
text: Three capabilities transform an LLM from a text generator into an agent: memory (persisting information beyond the context window), tools (calling external capabilities), and actions (operating in digital or physical environments). This section surveys the research landscape for each.

title: Memory, Tools & Actions
text:
Memory Systems

The Memory Problem
Standard LLMs have a fixed context window. For short conversations this is fine; for long-running agents tasked with multi-day work, it’s a fundamental limitation. Memory research asks: How do we give agents persistent, organized access to past experience?


Memory Taxonomy
Based on cognitive science (ACT-R, SOAR), the literature uses four memory types:









Type
Description
Stored As
Example




Working / In-context
Current context window
Tokens
Conversation history


Episodic
Time-stamped experiences
Natural language
“Yesterday I searched for X and found Y”


Semantic
World knowledge and facts
Embeddings / KV store
Entity facts, document summaries


Procedural
How-to skills and rules
Code / prompts
Voyager skill library, tool specs





Key Papers

MemGPT: Towards LLMs as Operating Systems (2023)
Packer et al. (UCB) · arXiv:2310.08560
The canonical memory paper. Draws analogy to OS virtual memory: a small “main context” (fast, limited) and large “external storage” (slow, vast). MemGPT manages movement between tiers, deciding what to load, store, and evict. Enables indefinite conversation and multi-document analysis.

Architecture: Hierarchical context management; interrupt-driven control flow; memory functions (search, append, update)
Results: Outperforms baselines on long-document tasks and multi-session conversation
GitHub: cpacker/MemGPT → became Letta



Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior (2023)
Park et al. · arXiv:2304.03442
Establishes the memory stream pattern: continuous natural language log of agent experiences. Three operations: observation (record events), reflection (synthesize into higher-level insights), planning (use memories to plan future actions). Recency, importance, and relevance jointly score which memories to retrieve.

Reflection: “I notice that I’ve talked to people about the party three times today — it must be important”
Impact: Most-cited memory architecture for autonomous agents



ReadAgent: Gist Memory for Long Documents (2024)
Lee et al. (Google) · arXiv:2402.09727
Mimics human reading: divide a document into episodes, compress each into a gist (short summary), retrieve details on demand. Extends effective context 3.5–20× without retraining.


Cognitive Architectures for Language Agents (2023)
Sumers et al. · arXiv:2309.02427
Grounds agent memory in cognitive science. Proposes four-part model (working, procedural, semantic, episodic) with consolidation and retrieval mechanisms.


A-MEM: Agentic Memory System (2025)
arXiv:2502.12110
Dynamic memory with Zettelkasten-inspired note organization. Creates interconnected notes with automatic linking and retrieval. Outperforms static memory systems on long-horizon tasks.


Mem0 (2024-2025)
mem0.ai · GitHub
Production memory layer for LLM applications. Extracts entities and relationships from conversations, stores them in a hybrid vector + graph database, and retrieves contextually relevant memories. Widely adopted in production agent systems.

title: Memory, Tools & Actions
text:
Tool Use & Function Calling

The Tool Use Revolution
Tools transform agents from text generators into actors: they can search the web, execute code, call APIs, query databases, send messages. The ability to use tools is what makes agents actually useful.


Foundational Papers

Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools (2023)
Schick et al. (Meta AI) · arXiv:2302.04761
First paper to show that LLMs can learn tool use in a self-supervised manner. The model generates candidate API calls, executes them, and keeps the ones that improve its own predictions. No large annotation dataset needed.

Tools: Calculator, search engine, Wikipedia, Q&A system, translator
Key insight: Tool use can be bootstrapped from the model itself



OpenAI Function Calling (June 2023)
OpenAI · Documentation
The most impactful practical milestone for tool use. Function calling standardized how LLMs invoke tools: define function schemas in JSON, model outputs structured JSON calls, application executes them. Became the dominant paradigm for production agent tool use.

Impact: Every major LLM provider now supports function calling (Claude tool use, Gemini function calling)
Parallel function calling (2023): Multiple tools called in a single pass



Gorilla: Large Language Model Connected with Massive APIs (2023)
Patil et al. (UCB) · arXiv:2305.15334
Fine-tuned model for API calling across 1,600+ APIs. Uses retrieval-augmented generation to select the right API. Demonstrates that retrieval + generation beats larger models for API calling.

Key insight: API calling is a retrieval problem as much as a generation problem
GitHub: ShishirPatil/gorilla



ToolLLM: Facilitating LLMs to Master 16000+ Real-World APIs (2023)
Qin et al. (Tsinghua) · arXiv:2307.16789
Dataset + model for calling 16,000+ RapidAPI endpoints. Introduces DFSDT (depth-first search-based decision tree) for robust tool selection and execution.


HuggingGPT / JARVIS (2023)
Shen et al. · arXiv:2303.17580
Uses ChatGPT to orchestrate hundreds of specialized ML models from HuggingFace Hub as tools. Planning → tool selection → execution → response synthesis.


RestGPT: Connecting LLMs with Real-World RESTful APIs (2023)
Song et al. · arXiv:2306.06624
Addresses the challenge of using real-world REST APIs (with complex schemas, authentication, pagination) from LLM agents.


Large Language Models as Tool Makers (2023)
Cai et al. · arXiv:2305.17126
Agents can create their own tools (as Python functions) and reuse them. Tool-making LLM generates tools; tool-using LLM employs them. Enables building up a library of reusable capabilities.


AnyTool: Self-Reflective, Hierarchical Agents for Tool Use (2024)
Du et al. · arXiv:2402.04253
Hierarchical tool selection with self-reflection for correcting wrong tool choices. State-of-the-art on ToolBench.


CodeAct: Executable Code Actions Elicit Better LLM Agents (2024)
Wang et al. · arXiv:2402.01030
Uses executable Python code as the action space rather than structured JSON tool calls. More flexible, handles complex multi-step logic natively, integrates with execution environments naturally.

title: Memory, Tools & Actions
text:
Action Spaces

Web Browsing Agents

WebGPT (2022)
Nakano et al. (OpenAI) · arXiv:2112.09332
GPT-3 fine-tuned to browse the web using a text-based interface. Searches, follows links, quotes sources. Trained with RLHF. The ancestor of all web-browsing agents.


Mind2Web: Towards a Generalist Agent for the Web (2023)
Deng et al. · arXiv:2306.06070
Dataset of 2,000 web tasks across 137 websites. Introduces the challenge of generalizing to new websites and tasks. Models must predict next action (click, type, scroll) from HTML + screenshot.


WebArena (2023)
Zhou et al. · arXiv:2307.13854
Fully reproducible web environment with 4 real applications. 812 tasks with executable evaluation. Critical benchmark for web agent progress.


WebAgent (Google, 2023)
Gur et al. · arXiv:2307.12856
Modular web agent: HTML summarization + task grounding + planning. Trained on real websites; demonstrates planning from natural language instructions.




GUI & Computer Use Agents
The 2024 frontier: agents controlling entire computers, not just browsers.

CogAgent: A Visual Language Model for GUI Agents (2024)
Hong et al. (Tsinghua) · arXiv:2312.08914
18B visual-language model trained on GUI navigation. Understands both screenshots and HTML. State-of-the-art on Mind2Web and AITW (Android in the Wild) benchmarks.


AppAgent: Multimodal Agents as Smartphone Users (2023)
Zhang et al. (Tencent) · arXiv:2312.13771
Autonomous agent for controlling smartphone apps via touch actions. Uses exploration phase (learns app UI) + deployment phase (executes tasks). No app-specific training.


UFO: A UI-Focused Agent for Windows OS Interaction (2024)
Zhang et al. (Microsoft) · arXiv:2402.07939
Agent for automating Windows OS tasks. Uses dual-agent design: HostAgent (application selection) + AppAgent (in-app navigation). Integrates Windows accessibility tree.


OS-Copilot: Towards Generalist Computer Agents on Linux (2024)
Wu et al. · arXiv:2402.07456
Generalist agent for Linux desktop. Self-directed skill library (FRIDAY); learns new skills through interaction; strong on GAIA and Spider2V benchmarks.


Anthropic Computer Use (October 2024)
Anthropic · Blog post
Claude 3.5 Sonnet with native ability to control computers: view screenshots, move mouse, click, type. Raised industry attention to computer-using agents as a near-term product category.


OSWorld Benchmark (2024)
Xie et al. · arXiv:2404.07972
369 real computer tasks across Ubuntu, Windows, macOS. Early systems scored <5%; by early 2025, Claude achieves 72.5%. Most comprehensive desktop agent benchmark.




Code Execution Agents

OpenDevin / OpenHands (2024)
All Hands AI · github.com/All-Hands-AI/OpenHands
Open-source coding agent with a sandboxed execution environment. Supports web browsing, file editing, code running. Mirrors Devin’s architecture but open-source.


SWE-agent (2024)
Yang et al. (Princeton) · arXiv:2405.15793
GitHub issue resolution agent. Custom Agent-Computer Interface (ACI) with file editing, search, execution tools designed specifically for software engineering. 12.29% on SWE-bench.




Embodied / Robotics Agents

SayCan: Grounding Language in Robotic Affordances (2022)
Ahn et al. (Google) · arXiv:2204.01691
LLM proposes high-level action plans; affordance model filters to physically feasible actions. Classic example of language grounding in robotics.


PaLM-E: An Embodied Multimodal Language Model (2023)
Driess et al. (Google) · arXiv:2303.03378
562B parameter multimodal model for embodied reasoning. Takes sensor inputs (images, state estimates) and generates text + robot actions. Demonstrates transfer from language tasks to robot control.

title: Memory, Tools & Actions
text:
Retrieval-Augmented Agents (Agentic RAG)
RAG for agents extends beyond simple document retrieval to active, query-planning retrieval:

Self-RAG: Learning to Retrieve, Generate, and Critique (2023)
Asai et al. · arXiv:2310.11511
The model decides when to retrieve (not on every query), critiques retrieved passages, and generates with inline citations. Four special tokens (Retrieve, ISREL, ISSUP, ISUSE) gate retrieval and quality.


Agentic RAG Patterns (2024)
Practitioner pattern: agent loops that decompose queries, retrieve in multiple rounds, verify completeness, and synthesize. Distinguished from basic RAG by active query planning.

text:

Continue to 2024–2026 Frontier →

title: Reasoning & Planning
text:
Reasoning and planning are the cognitive core of LLM agents. This area has evolved dramatically: from simple chain-of-thought prompting (2022) to structured tree search (2023), to RL-trained reasoning models (2024-2025) that discover reasoning strategies from scratch.
The central questions:

How can LLMs be prompted to reason step-by-step?
How can reasoning be structured (trees, graphs, hierarchies)?
How can agents self-improve via feedback?
How do we scale reasoning with test-time compute?
How can agents plan over long horizons?

title: Reasoning & Planning
text:
Reasoning and planning are the cognitive core of LLM agents. This area has evolved dramatically: from simple chain-of-thought prompting (2022) to structured tree search (2023), to RL-trained reasoning models (2024-2025) that discover reasoning strategies from scratch.
The central questions:

How can LLMs be prompted to reason step-by-step?
How can reasoning be structured (trees, graphs, hierarchies)?
How can agents self-improve via feedback?
How do we scale reasoning with test-time compute?
How can agents plan over long horizons?

title: Reasoning & Planning
text:
Chain-of-Thought: The Foundation

Chain-of-Thought Prompting (2022)
Wei et al. · arXiv:2201.11903 · NeurIPS 2022
The root of all LLM reasoning work. Providing step-by-step reasoning examples in the prompt dramatically improves performance on arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic tasks. An emergent capability — most powerful in models ≥100B parameters.

Result: 540B model with 8 CoT examples achieves SOTA on GSM8K math



Zero-Shot Chain-of-Thought (2022)
Kojima et al. · arXiv:2205.11916 · NeurIPS 2022
Astonishing finding: adding “Let’s think step by step” to a prompt, with no examples, elicits multi-step reasoning. Simpler than few-shot CoT but often nearly as effective.


Self-Consistency (2022)
Wang et al. · arXiv:2203.11171 · ICLR 2023
Instead of greedy decoding, sample multiple diverse reasoning paths and take a majority vote. Multiple paths to the same answer increase confidence. Significant gains on arithmetic and commonsense tasks.


Least-to-Most Prompting (2022)
Zhou et al. · arXiv:2205.10625
Decompose a hard problem into easier sub-problems, then solve them sequentially, each building on the previous. Better generalization than standard CoT on compositional tasks.

title: Reasoning & Planning
section: Structured Reasoning: Trees, Graphs, and Beyond
text:
Structured Reasoning: Trees, Graphs, and Beyond

Tree of Thoughts (2023)
Yao et al. · arXiv:2305.10601 · NeurIPS 2023
Major advance over CoT. Treats problem-solving as tree search. The model generates multiple candidate “thoughts” (intermediate reasoning steps), evaluates each, and uses BFS or DFS to explore promising branches. Enables backtracking and lookahead.

Game of 24: 4% (CoT) → 74% (ToT)
Key ideas: Thought decomposition; self-evaluation of intermediate states; deliberate exploration
GitHub: princeton-nlp/tree-of-thought-llm



Graph of Thoughts (2023)
Besta et al. · arXiv:2308.09687 · AAAI 2024
Extends Tree of Thoughts to arbitrary graph structures: thoughts can merge (aggregate), loop, or branch in non-tree patterns. More expressive; outperforms ToT on sorting tasks (+62%) with lower compute.


Algorithm of Thoughts (2023)
Sel et al. · arXiv:2308.10379
Encodes classic algorithms (DFS, BFS) directly into the reasoning trace. The LLM follows a structured algorithmic process, improving reliability on search and optimization problems.


ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting (2023)
Yao et al. · arXiv:2210.03629 · ICLR 2023
Interleaves reasoning traces with tool-use actions. The model thinks about what to do, acts, observes results, and thinks again. The canonical agent reasoning loop.
(See Foundations for full entry)

title: Reasoning & Planning
text:
Planning Architectures

LLM+P: Combining LLMs with Classical Planning (2023)
Liu et al. · arXiv:2304.11477 · ICML 2024
LLMs translate natural language problem descriptions into PDDL (formal planning language), then classical planners (guaranteed optimal) solve them. Combines LLM flexibility with planning rigor.

Key insight: LLMs are good at understanding; classical planners are good at optimizing



Plan-and-Execute Agents (2023)
Chase / LangChain · Blog post
Separate the planner (generates a high-level multi-step plan) from the executor (carries out each step). Cleaner architecture than monolithic ReAct; easier to monitor and debug.


DEPS: Describe, Explain, Plan and Select (2023)
Wang et al. · arXiv:2302.01560
Interactive planning framework for open-world agents (Minecraft). Uses natural language descriptions and explanations to guide selection of sub-tasks, with error recovery.


Inner Monologue: Embodied Reasoning via Language Feedback (2022)
Huang et al. · arXiv:2207.05608 · CoRL 2022
Robots form “inner monologues” — natural language reasoning about failures, informed by environment feedback. Iterative plan refinement based on real-world observations.


Reasoning via Planning (RAP) (2023)
Hao et al. · arXiv:2305.14992
Uses the LLM as both a world model and a reasoning agent. Monte Carlo Tree Search over a space of reasoning actions, with the LLM evaluating states. Outperforms CoT and ToT on mathematical reasoning.


ADaPT: As-Needed Decomposition and Planning (2023)
Trivedi et al. · arXiv:2311.05772 · NAACL 2024
Recursive decomposition that re-decomposes sub-tasks if they fail. Addresses brittleness of fixed decomposition plans.


Step-Back Prompting (2023)
Zou et al. · arXiv:2310.06117 · ICLR 2024
Derives high-level abstractions and first principles before solving specific instances. 42.8% improvement on complex multi-hop QA. “Step back, think at the abstract level, then solve.”


Decomposed Prompting (2022)
Khot et al. · arXiv:2210.02406 · EMNLP 2022
Breaks complex tasks into modular sub-prompts, each solving a specific sub-task. Enables debugging and composition.

title: Reasoning & Planning
text:
Reflection & Self-Improvement
A major theme of 2023: agents that evaluate and improve their own outputs.

Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning (2023)
Shinn et al. · arXiv:2303.11366 · NeurIPS 2023
Agents generate verbal reflections on their failures, stored in an episodic memory buffer. On the next attempt, the agent incorporates its own critique. No gradient updates — reinforcement via language alone.

Results: HumanEval coding: 67% → 91% (GPT-4); WebArena significant improvements
Key ideas: Verbal reinforcement; episodic memory of failures; trial-and-learn loop
GitHub: noahshinn/reflexion



Self-Refine: Iterative Refinement with Self-Feedback (2023)
Madaan et al. · arXiv:2303.17651 · NeurIPS 2023
Single model generates output, critiques it, then revises it — iteratively. Works across code, dialogue, math, essay writing. No additional training data needed.

Key insight: Same model can generate and critique; iteration improves quality



CRITIC: Large Language Models Can Self-Correct with Tool-Interactive Critiquing (2023)
Gou et al. · arXiv:2305.11738 · ICLR 2024
Extends self-critique by grounding verification in tool use — uses web search, code execution, or calculators to verify claims, then corrects based on evidence.

Key insight: Tool-grounded critique is more reliable than pure self-evaluation



Constitutional AI / Self-Critique (2022)
Bai et al. (Anthropic) · arXiv:2212.08073
Uses a set of principles (“constitution”) to guide the model in critiquing and revising its outputs. Foundation for AI alignment work with implications for agent safety.

title: Reasoning & Planning
text:
Search-Based Planning

Monte Carlo Tree Search for LLM Reasoning (2024)
Multiple papers explore MCTS over reasoning traces:

Scaling LLM Test-Time Compute (Snell et al., 2024) · arXiv:2408.03314 — Shows targeted test-time compute scaling outperforms using a larger model
RAP with Monte Carlo (Hao et al., 2023) — MCTS over world-model states
MCTS+Reflexion combinations explored in 2024



Large Language Monkeys: Sampling and Majority Vote (2024)
Brown et al. · arXiv:2407.21787
Repeated sampling with majority vote reveals that coverage (any correct solution found) scales well with samples, even when individual attempts are low probability. Implications for test-time compute allocation.

title: Reasoning & Planning
text:
Reasoning Models: RL for Reasoning (2024-2025)
A paradigm shift: instead of prompting strategies, train the model to reason using reinforcement learning.

OpenAI o1 / o3 (2024-2025)
OpenAI · Technical report
Models trained with RL to produce long internal “chains of thought” before answering. Dramatically improves performance on math, coding, and scientific reasoning. Marks the transition from “prompting for reasoning” to “models that reason natively.”

o1 benchmark: Near-human performance on Olympiad math; 89th percentile on competitive programming
o3: Solves 25% of FrontierMath problems; high ARC-AGI-1 scores
Implication for agents: Reasoning capability is now a first-class model feature



DeepSeek-R1 (2025)
DeepSeek AI · arXiv:2501.12948
Open-source reasoning model trained with GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization). Shows that RL-based reasoning can be achieved without supervised reasoning traces — the model discovers reasoning strategies from scratch.

Key insight: Reasoning emerges from RL reward; “aha moments” observed in training
Impact: First open-weight model matching o1-level reasoning on math



s1: Simple Test-Time Scaling (2025)
Muennighoff et al. · arXiv:2501.19393
Shows that even a small, fine-tuned open model can match o1 performance on math reasoning by training on a curated set of 1,000 “hard” reasoning problems. Budget forcing via extended thinking tokens.

title: Reasoning & Planning
text:
World Models for Planning

Language Models as World Models (2023)
Research thread exploring LLMs as simulators of environment dynamics for planning purposes.

Reasoning via Planning (RAP) — explicit world model use for tree search
WorldGPT — generating world simulations for agent planning
Long-horizon planning remains an open problem; world models help bridge the gap

title: Reasoning & Planning
text:
Benchmarks for Reasoning & Planning

title: Reasoning & Planning
text:
Key Concepts & Taxonomy

Reasoning Paradigms








Paradigm
Description
Strength




Chain-of-Thought
Linear reasoning trace
Simple, widely applicable


Self-Consistency
Majority vote over multiple paths
Robustness, reliability


Tree of Thoughts
Branching search over thoughts
Complex problems, backtracking


ReAct
Interleaved reasoning + action
Tool use, grounded reasoning


Reflexion
Verbal RL from failure
Iterative improvement


RL Reasoning (o1)
Trained chain-of-thought
Deep, complex reasoning





Planning Strategies



Strategy
When to Use




Direct
Simple tasks, clear actions


Plan-and-Execute
Multi-step tasks, need structure


Hierarchical
Long-horizon tasks, sub-goal decomposition


Reflective/Iterative
Tasks with verifiable outcomes


Search-Based
Complex optimization, multiple valid paths




Continue to Multi-Agent Systems →

title: Taxonomy & Conceptual Framework
text:
An LLM agent is a system in which a large language model serves as a central reasoning engine, capable of taking sequences of actions toward a goal — not merely producing a single response. The key distinctions from standard LLM use:

Agency over time: The model takes multiple steps, observes results, and adapts
Tool use: The model can invoke external capabilities (search, code execution, APIs)
Goal-directedness: The system pursues an objective, not just answers a question
Memory beyond context: The system can store and retrieve information across turns

The canonical formulation (from Wang et al., 2023 survey): an agent has a Brain (the LLM), Perception (inputs), Memory (short- and long-term storage), Action (what it can do), and Planning (how it decides what to do).

title: Taxonomy & Conceptual Framework
text:
An LLM agent is a system in which a large language model serves as a central reasoning engine, capable of taking sequences of actions toward a goal — not merely producing a single response. The key distinctions from standard LLM use:

Agency over time: The model takes multiple steps, observes results, and adapts
Tool use: The model can invoke external capabilities (search, code execution, APIs)
Goal-directedness: The system pursues an objective, not just answers a question
Memory beyond context: The system can store and retrieve information across turns

The canonical formulation (from Wang et al., 2023 survey): an agent has a Brain (the LLM), Perception (inputs), Memory (short- and long-term storage), Action (what it can do), and Planning (how it decides what to do).

title: Taxonomy & Conceptual Framework
text:
The Core Agent Loop
Most LLM agent architectures implement some version of this loop:
Observe → Think → Act → Observe → ...
This was crystallized in ReAct (Yao et al., 2023):
Thought: I need to find X
Action: search("X")
Observation: [result]
Thought: Now I know X, I should do Y
Action: ...
Variations on this loop form the backbone of nearly every agent system.

title: Taxonomy & Conceptual Framework
text:
Decomposing Agent Design Space
We can decompose the design space of LLM agents along five key dimensions:

1. 🧠 Reasoning / Planning
How does the agent decide what to do next?








Approach
Description
Examples




Direct
Single-step action selection
Basic tool-use with function calling


Chain-of-Thought
Linear reasoning trace
ReAct, CoT prompting


Tree/Graph Search
Branching exploration
Tree of Thoughts, MCTS agents


Hierarchical
Decompose → solve subgoals
Plan-and-Execute, HierAgent


Reflective
Evaluate → revise plans
Reflexion, Self-Refine, CRITIC





2. 💾 Memory Architecture
What information can the agent access and how?








Type
Description
Examples




In-context
Everything in the prompt window
Standard LLM, short conversations


External (vector)
Retrieved from embedding store
RAG, MemGPT external storage


Episodic
Record of past events
MemGPT, Generative Agents diary


Semantic
Facts about the world
Knowledge graph integration


Procedural
How-to skills, code
Voyager skill library





3. 🔧 Action Space
What can the agent actually do?



Category
Examples




Search/Web
Google search, web browsing, Wikipedia


Code execution
Python REPL, shell, Jupyter


API calls
REST APIs, function calling


File I/O
Read/write files


GUI/Computer
Click, type, screenshot


Agent spawning
Spawn sub-agents, delegate tasks


Communication
Send messages, emails





4. 🤝 Agent Multiplicity
Is the system a single agent or multiple?








Pattern
Description
Examples




Single
One LLM does everything
Standard ReAct agents


Specialist pipeline
Sequential specialization
HuggingGPT, modular agents


Peer collaboration
Agents debate and refine
CAMEL, SPP


Hierarchical
Manager + workers
AutoGen, MetaGPT, ChatDev


Society
Many autonomous agents
Generative Agents, AgentVerse





5. 🎯 Degree of Autonomy
How much human oversight is in the loop?



Level
Description




Tool-augmented
LLM with tools, human-in-the-loop


Semi-autonomous
Agent acts, human approves key steps


Fully autonomous
Agent runs until task complete


Multi-hop autonomous
Long-horizon, fully unsupervised

title: Taxonomy & Conceptual Framework
text:
A Taxonomy of Agent Architectures
Based on the literature, we can identify several major architectural families:

Family 1: Tool-Augmented LLMs
“Give the LLM tools to call”
The simplest agent pattern. A single LLM is given a set of callable tools. It reasons about which tool to call and synthesizes results. The focus is on tool integration more than autonomous goal-pursuit.
Key papers: Toolformer, MRKL, OpenAI function calling, Claude tool use Key frameworks: LangChain tools, OpenAI Assistants


Family 2: ReAct-Style Agents
“Interleave reasoning and acting”
The agent alternates between Thought (reasoning about what to do) and Action (doing it). The observation from the action feeds back into the next thought. This is the dominant pattern for task-solving agents.
Key papers: ReAct, DEPS, Inner Monologue Key frameworks: LangChain agents, LlamaIndex agents


Family 3: Plan-Then-Execute
“Make a plan, then execute it”
A planner agent generates a high-level plan, then an executor carries out each step. Allows more structured task decomposition and is easier to monitor.
Key papers: Plan-and-Execute (Chase, 2023), LLM+P, DEPS Key frameworks: LangGraph, LangChain Plan-and-Execute


Family 4: Reflective / Self-Improving Agents
“Learn from mistakes within a task”
Agents that evaluate their own performance and update their approach. Introduces a critic or reflection component.
Key papers: Reflexion, Self-Refine, CRITIC, Constitutional AI Key frameworks: AutoGen (with feedback), LangGraph with loops


Family 5: Multi-Agent Systems
“Specialized agents collaborating”
Multiple LLM instances with different roles (planner, coder, critic, etc.) communicate and collaborate to solve complex tasks.
Key papers: CAMEL, MetaGPT, ChatDev, AutoGen, AgentVerse Key frameworks: AutoGen, CrewAI, LangGraph multi-agent


Family 6: Memory-Augmented Agents
“Agents with persistent, structured memory”
Go beyond the context window with external memory systems: vector databases, structured stores, memory hierarchies modeled after cognitive architectures.
Key papers: MemGPT, Generative Agents, A-MEM, ReadAgent Key frameworks: MemGPT, LangChain Memory


Family 7: Embodied / Action Agents
“Agents that act in the physical or digital world”
Agents that control computers (GUI agents), robots (embodied agents), or execute code. The action space is rich and grounded.
Key papers: SayCan, RT-2, CogAgent, UFO, OS-Copilot, SWE-agent Key products: Devin, Claude Computer Use, OpenAI Operator

title: Taxonomy & Conceptual Framework
text:
Key Design Tensions
The literature reveals several recurring tensions that different systems resolve differently:







Tension
Tradeoff




Autonomy vs. Oversight
More autonomy = more capability, more risk


Generality vs. Specialization
Specialist agents perform better, general agents are more flexible


In-context vs. External memory
Context is fast but limited; external is vast but retrieval is lossy


Natural language vs. Structured
Natural language is flexible; structured is reliable


Single vs. Multi-agent
Multi-agent enables specialization; single-agent is simpler to debug


Plan-first vs. Interleave
Planning enables lookahead; interleaving enables reactivity

title: Taxonomy & Conceptual Framework
text:
How the Field Has Evolved








Era
Dominant Pattern
Key Innovation




2022
Tool-augmented LLMs
MRKL, WebGPT, SayCan — LLMs + specialized modules


Early 2023
ReAct agents
Interleaved reasoning + action; Toolformer


Mid 2023
Autonomous agents
AutoGPT, BabyAGI — long-horizon goal pursuit


Late 2023
Multi-agent + Memory
MetaGPT, AutoGen, MemGPT, Generative Agents


2024
Coding agents + Infra
SWE-agent, Devin; LangGraph, CrewAI


2025-2026
Agentic products + MCP
Claude Computer Use, Operator; model-native tooling

title: LLM Agents: A Survey of Architectures & Designs (2022–2026)
text:
A thorough review of the landscape of LLM-based agent architectures, designs, and the evolving ecosystem — covering papers, preprints, frameworks, blog posts, and open-source tools.
Compiled March 2026 · View on GitHub

title: LLM Agents: A Survey of Architectures & Designs (2022–2026)
text:
What Is This?
This survey maps the landscape of Large Language Model (LLM) based agents — systems where LLMs serve not just as text generators, but as reasoning engines that can plan, use tools, access memory, and take actions in the world.
From 2022 to 2026, the field has exploded. This guide aims to be a navigable map: organized by theme, covering both the academic literature and the practitioner ecosystem.



4+


Years Covered




200+


Papers & Resources




6


Major Themes




2022–2026


Time Range

title: LLM Agents: A Survey of Architectures & Designs (2022–2026)
text:
Navigate the Survey



🗺️


Taxonomy & Framework
A conceptual map of the field: how to decompose and categorize LLM agents. The core components, design dimensions, and how the major architectures fit together.




🏛️


Foundations (2022–2023)
The papers and ideas that established the field. ReAct, Toolformer, HuggingGPT, WebGPT, and the early agent taxonomies.




🧠


Reasoning & Planning
How agents think: Chain-of-Thought, Tree of Thoughts, Reflexion, search-based planning, task decomposition, and self-improvement.




🤝


Multi-Agent Systems
Multiple agents working together: CAMEL, AutoGen, MetaGPT, ChatDev, role specialization, orchestration patterns, and evaluation benchmarks.




🧰


Memory, Tools & Actions
How agents remember and act: memory architectures, tool use (Toolformer, function calling), action spaces, retrieval-augmented agents, and computer use.




🚀


2024–2026: Frontier
The leading edge: coding agents (SWE-agent, Devin), agentic products (Operator, Computer Use), MCP, o1/o3 implications, safety, and where things are heading.

title: LLM Agents: A Survey of Architectures & Designs (2022–2026)
text:
How to Use This Survey

New to agents? Start with Taxonomy, then Foundations
Researcher? Each section has structured paper entries with links and summaries
Practitioner? Check Multi-Agent Systems for frameworks and 2024–2026 for the current ecosystem
Looking for resources? See Resources & Links for a curated reading list

title: LLM Agents: A Survey of Architectures & Designs (2022–2026)
text:
Scope & Methodology
This survey covers:

Papers & preprints (arXiv, ACL, NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, etc.)
Technical blog posts from researchers and practitioners
Open-source frameworks and tools (GitHub)
Time range: 2022 through March 2026

The focus is on LLM-based agents — systems using large language models as a core reasoning component. We touch on embodied/robotics agents when they are directly relevant, but the primary focus is on digital, language-model-centric work.

This survey was compiled in March 2026. The field moves fast — if you spot a missing reference, open an issue.

title: Foundations (2022–2023)
text:
The modern LLM agent field traces to a cluster of papers from 2022–2023 that established the core paradigms still in use today. Before these works, LLMs were primarily used as single-shot text generators. This period showed they could act — using tools, browsing the web, executing code, and reasoning across multiple steps toward a goal.
The foundational insight: an LLM becomes an agent when you give it a loop — the ability to observe outcomes of its actions and decide what to do next.

title: Foundations (2022–2023)
text:
The modern LLM agent field traces to a cluster of papers from 2022–2023 that established the core paradigms still in use today. Before these works, LLMs were primarily used as single-shot text generators. This period showed they could act — using tools, browsing the web, executing code, and reasoning across multiple steps toward a goal.
The foundational insight: an LLM becomes an agent when you give it a loop — the ability to observe outcomes of its actions and decide what to do next.

title: Foundations (2022–2023)
text:
Survey & Taxonomy Papers
These surveys provide the broadest maps of the field:

A Survey on Large Language Model based Autonomous Agents (2023)
Wang et al. · arXiv:2308.11432 · Frontiers of Computer Science, 2024
The most comprehensive survey of the field. Covers 200+ papers and presents a unified framework decomposing agents into four pillars: Brain (LLM), Perception, Memory, and Action. Analyzes applications across social science, natural science, and engineering. Actively maintained through v7 (March 2025).

Key ideas: Unified architecture spanning majority of prior work; systematic taxonomy by application domain; evaluation methodology discussion
Impact: De facto reference paper for new entrants to the field



The Rise and Potential of Large Language Model Based Agents (2023)
Xi et al. (30 co-authors) · arXiv:2309.07864 · Science China Information Sciences, 2025
An 86-page survey tracing agents from philosophical origins through modern AI. Uses a brain-perception-action framework. Explores single-agent, multi-agent, and human-agent cooperation. Discusses agent societies, emergent behaviors, and the path toward AGI.

Key ideas: Historical and philosophical grounding; vision for agent societies; multi-agent coordination patterns



Agent AI: Surveying the Horizons of Multimodal Interaction (2024)
Wang et al. · arXiv:2401.03568
Expands scope to multimodal agents operating across text, vision, and action spaces. Covers gaming, robotics, healthcare, GUI navigation. A useful bridge between language-only and embodied/multimodal agents.


Large Language Model Agent: A Survey on Methodology, Applications and Challenges (2025)
Luo et al. · arXiv:2503.21460
Most recent comprehensive survey (March 2025) with structured taxonomy for LLM agent methodology. Catalogs current applications and synthesizes open challenges.


Cognitive Architectures for Language Agents (2023)
Sumers et al. · arXiv:2309.02427
Grounds LLM agent design in cognitive science. Proposes four-part memory model (working, procedural, semantic, episodic) drawing from ACT-R and SOAR. Essential for understanding memory system design.

title: Foundations (2022–2023)
text:
Core Foundational Papers

ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models (2023)
Yao et al. · arXiv:2210.03629 · ICLR 2023
The most cited and influential agent paper. Introduces the ReAct paradigm: interleaved reasoning traces and actions. The agent alternates between Thought: (reasoning about what to do) and Action: (doing it), with Observation: feeding back the result. This loop is the skeleton of most modern agent systems.
Thought: I need to find the population of France.
Action: search("France population 2024")
Observation: France has a population of approximately 68 million.
Thought: Now I can answer the question.
Action: finish("approximately 68 million")

Key ideas: Reasoning and acting are synergistic, not separate; grounding in external feedback reduces hallucination; enables error correction mid-task
Results: Outperforms pure CoT and pure acting on HotpotQA, FEVER, ALFWorld, WebShop



Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models (2022)
Wei et al. · arXiv:2201.11903 · NeurIPS 2022
The prerequisite to ReAct. Shows that prompting LLMs to generate intermediate reasoning steps dramatically improves performance on arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning. Established that LLMs can reason step-by-step — the cognitive foundation for all agent work.

Key ideas: Few-shot CoT prompting; emergent capability (most effective at 100B+ params); generalizes across reasoning domains



Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools (2023)
Schick et al. · arXiv:2302.04761
Shows how to train an LLM to use tools in a self-supervised manner. The model learns to insert API calls into its own generated text, execute them, and incorporate results — without large human-annotated datasets.

Tools learned: Calculator, Wikipedia search, calendar, Q&A system, translator
Key ideas: Self-supervised tool learning; decides which API to call, when, and with what arguments; training signal from self-generated examples
Legacy: Prefigures the function-calling paradigm now standard in GPT-4, Claude, etc.



MRKL Systems: A Modular, Neuro-Symbolic Architecture (2022)
Karpas et al. · arXiv:2205.00445
Early vision of modular AI where an LLM router dispatches to specialized expert modules (calculators, databases, ML models). One of the first papers to articulate the “LLM + tools” architecture.

Key ideas: LLM as orchestrator; discrete symbolic modules for reliable computation; router learns which module is appropriate
Legacy: Direct ancestor of today’s tool-calling agents



WebGPT: Browser-Assisted Question Answering (2022)
Nakano et al. · arXiv:2112.09332
GPT-3 fine-tuned to browse the web to answer questions. Introduced the concept of browser-using agents — now a major category. Used RLHF to train the browsing behavior.

Key ideas: Web browsing as an action space; RLHF for agent behavior; long-form question answering with citations



HuggingGPT / JARVIS (2023)
Shen et al. · arXiv:2303.17580
LLM (ChatGPT) as a task planner that orchestrates hundreds of specialized ML models from HuggingFace. The LLM parses user requests, selects the right ML models, executes them in sequence, and synthesizes results.

Key ideas: LLM as controller of a model hub; structured task planning; multi-modal capability via model composition
GitHub: microsoft/JARVIS



SayCan: Grounding Language in Robotic Affordances (2022)
Ahn et al. · arXiv:2204.01691
Landmark paper combining LLMs with robotics. The LLM generates possible action plans; a learned “affordance” model scores which actions are physically feasible in the current environment. Early example of embodied agent planning.

Key ideas: LLM proposes, affordance model filters; language grounding in physical world; “what is both useful AND possible”



Voyager: An Open-Ended Embodied Agent in Minecraft (2023)
Wang et al. · arXiv:2305.16291
GPT-4 playing Minecraft autonomously for indefinite time spans. Features a procedural skill library that grows over time, an automatic curriculum for proposing increasingly complex tasks, and iterative skill refinement via execution feedback.

Key ideas: Lifelong learning via skill accumulation; automatic curriculum; code-as-action (skills written as JavaScript); never “forgets” learned skills
Results: 3× faster at milestones, unlocks tech tree substantially faster than prior methods



AutoGPT (2023)
Riedl et al. (community) · GitHub
Not a paper, but arguably the most influential artifact of 2023. AutoGPT showed the world what a fully autonomous LLM agent looked like: give it a goal, let it run. It sparked massive public interest and downstream research, despite significant reliability issues.

Key ideas: Autonomous goal pursuit; persistent memory; web search + code execution; iterative task decomposition
Legacy: Demonstrated demand for autonomous agents; exposed failure modes that drove later research



BabyAGI (2023)
Nakajima · GitHub
Minimalist task management agent. Maintains a task list, executes tasks with an LLM + web search, and adds new tasks based on results. Showed that a simple agent loop could produce surprising emergent behaviors.

title: Foundations (2022–2023)
text:
Standard Agent Decomposition
Based on the survey literature, the field converged on this standard decomposition:








Component
Description
Examples




Perception
Inputs from environment
Text, images, tool outputs, web pages


Memory
Information storage & retrieval
In-context, vector DB, key-value stores


Planning
Deciding what to do
ReAct, CoT, hierarchical decomposition


Action
Executing decisions
Tool calls, web search, code execution


Learning
Improving over time
Reflexion, skill libraries, RLHF

text:

Continue to Reasoning & Planning →

title: Multi-Agent Systems & Frameworks
text:
When a single LLM agent reaches its limits — context length, expertise breadth, task complexity — multi-agent systems offer a solution: multiple specialized agents that collaborate, critique, and coordinate. This area exploded in 2023 and matured into production-ready infrastructure by 2025.
Key themes: - Role specialization: Planner, Executor, Critic, Coder, Tester — each agent does what it’s best at - Communication protocols: Natural language messages → structured schemas → MCP - Framework consolidation: From dozens of early frameworks to a handful of dominant players - Emergent behaviors: Both positive (division of labor) and negative (groupthink, error propagation)

title: Multi-Agent Systems & Frameworks
text:
When a single LLM agent reaches its limits — context length, expertise breadth, task complexity — multi-agent systems offer a solution: multiple specialized agents that collaborate, critique, and coordinate. This area exploded in 2023 and matured into production-ready infrastructure by 2025.
Key themes: - Role specialization: Planner, Executor, Critic, Coder, Tester — each agent does what it’s best at - Communication protocols: Natural language messages → structured schemas → MCP - Framework consolidation: From dozens of early frameworks to a handful of dominant players - Emergent behaviors: Both positive (division of labor) and negative (groupthink, error propagation)

title: Multi-Agent Systems & Frameworks
text:
Foundational Multi-Agent Papers

Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior (2023)
Park et al. (Stanford) · arXiv:2304.03442 · UIST 2023 Best Paper
The landmark demonstration: 25 LLM agents inhabiting a simulated town (“Smallville”), forming relationships, planning their days, and exhibiting emergent social behaviors. Each agent has a memory stream (natural language records of experiences), reflection (synthesizing higher-level insights), and planning (daily schedules, goal-directed behavior).

Emergent behaviors: Spontaneous party planning, rumor spreading, relationship formation
Memory architecture: Observation → memory stream → reflection → planning
Key insight: Rich social behaviors emerge from individual memory + reflection + planning
Impact: Established the template for memory-rich multi-agent systems



CAMEL: Communicative Agents for “Mind” Exploration (2023)
Li et al. (KAUST) · arXiv:2303.17760 · NeurIPS 2023
Proposes role-playing as the mechanism for multi-agent cooperation. Two agents (user and assistant) are given roles and an objective; they converse autonomously without human input. Introduces inception prompting to initialize and maintain role coherence.

Key ideas: Autonomous role-playing; inception prompting; generates high-quality conversation datasets
GitHub: camel-ai/camel



MetaGPT: Meta Programming for a Generalizable Agent System (2023)
Hong et al. · arXiv:2308.00352 · ICLR 2024
Applies software engineering best practices to multi-agent coordination. Agents play roles from a software company (Product Manager, Architect, Engineer, QA). They work from shared documents (PRD, architecture, API spec), reducing miscommunication.

Key ideas: Structured document-based coordination; SOP (standard operating procedures) for agents; shared workspace; role → deliverable mapping
Results: Outperforms prior SOTA on HumanEval and MBPP; lower cost per task
GitHub: geekan/MetaGPT · 50k+ stars



ChatDev: Communicative Agents for Software Development (2023)
Qian et al. (Tsinghua) · arXiv:2307.07924 · ACL 2024
Simulates a full software development company with specialized agents (CEO, CTO, Programmer, Reviewer, Tester). Communication uses both natural language and code. Demonstrates that structured agent communication produces better software than single-agent generation.

Key ideas: Phase-gated development (design → coding → testing → documentation); role-based SOPs; communicative hallucination detection
GitHub: OpenBMB/ChatDev



AutoGen: Enabling Next-Gen LLM Applications via Multi-Agent Conversation (2023)
Wu et al. (Microsoft) · arXiv:2308.08155
Most widely adopted multi-agent framework. Introduces conversable agents: any two agents can converse, with rich customization of termination conditions, human-in-the-loop integration, and tool use. Defines a flexible conversation topology that supports many multi-agent patterns.

Key ideas: Conversable agent abstraction; flexible conversation patterns; code execution integration; GroupChat for multi-party conversations
GitHub: microsoft/autogen · 40k+ stars



AgentVerse: Facilitating Multi-Agent Collaboration (2023)
Chen et al. (Tsinghua) · arXiv:2308.10848
Platform for studying emergent behaviors in multi-agent systems. Systematically investigates both positive (division of labor, complementary expertise) and negative (groupthink, error amplification) emergent phenomena.

GitHub: OpenBMB/AgentVerse



DyLAN: Dynamic LLM-Powered Agent Network (2023)
Liu et al. · arXiv:2310.02170
Dynamic multi-agent network where agent connectivity evolves based on task requirements. Introduces agent importance scoring and dynamic team formation.


Solo Performance Prompting (SPP) (2023)
Wang et al. · arXiv:2307.05300
Simulates multi-agent collaboration with a single LLM: the model plays multiple personas sequentially. Achieves similar benefits to actual multi-agent systems with lower cost.

Key insight: Multiple perspectives from a single model improve factuality and reasoning



AgentScope (2024)
Alibaba · arXiv:2402.14034 · GitHub
Production-grade multi-agent platform from Alibaba. Focuses on fault tolerance, distributed execution, and scalability. Designed for real-world deployment with hundreds of concurrent agents.

title: Multi-Agent Systems & Frameworks
text:
Open-Source Frameworks

LangChain
github.com/langchain-ai/langchain · 100k+ stars
The dominant general-purpose agent framework. Provides abstractions for agents, tools, memory, chains, and retrieval. Agent module supports ReAct, structured chat, function-calling, and custom agents. Huge ecosystem of integrations.

Architecture: Chain-based composition; LCEL (LangChain Expression Language) for declarative pipelines; extensive tool library
Best for: Rapid prototyping; connecting to many data sources and APIs



LangGraph
github.com/langchain-ai/langgraph · 15k+ stars
Graph-based agent workflow framework built on LangChain. Agents are nodes; state flows along edges. Supports cycles (essential for iterative agent loops), conditional branching, and persistent state across steps.

Architecture: Stateful graph; explicit control flow; checkpointing; human-in-the-loop support
Best for: Complex multi-step agents; workflows needing explicit state management; production deployment
v1.0 released: November 2025



AutoGen
github.com/microsoft/autogen · 40k+ stars
Microsoft’s multi-agent conversation framework. Define agents with roles and capabilities; they converse to solve tasks. Supports GroupChat (multiple agents), code execution, and human proxy agents.

Architecture: Conversable agent base class; message-passing; human-in-the-loop via proxy agent
Best for: Multi-agent conversations; research; code-executing agents



CrewAI
github.com/joaomdmoura/crewAI · 25k+ stars
Role-based agent framework. Define a crew of agents, each with a role, goal, and backstory. Assign tasks; agents collaborate sequentially or in parallel.

Architecture: Agent → Task → Crew; clean role abstractions; process types (sequential, hierarchical)
Best for: Business-oriented multi-agent workflows; readable, maintainable crew definitions



DSPy
github.com/stanfordnlp/dspy · 20k+ stars
Programming model for LLMs. Instead of writing prompts, you write modules with signatures. DSPy optimizes prompts/few-shot examples automatically. Includes agent-capable modules (ReAct, Chain-of-Thought).

Architecture: Modules with typed signatures; compilers that optimize prompts; separation of logic from prompting
Best for: Systematic prompt optimization; research; building reliable LM pipelines



Semantic Kernel
github.com/microsoft/semantic-kernel · 22k+ stars
Microsoft’s enterprise-grade agent SDK. Supports .NET, Python, Java. Strong focus on plugin architecture (tools as plugins) and integration with Azure AI services.

Best for: Enterprise .NET/Azure environments; production-grade tool integration



Haystack
github.com/deepset-ai/haystack · 17k+ stars
Production-focused NLP framework with strong RAG + agent support. Component-based pipeline architecture. Excellent for document-heavy agent applications.


OpenAI Swarm (2024)
github.com/openai/swarm · experimental
OpenAI’s lightweight multi-agent framework. Minimal abstraction: agents + handoffs. Designed for clarity and educational purposes.

title: Multi-Agent Systems & Frameworks
text:
Orchestration Patterns
The literature reveals recurring multi-agent patterns:








Pattern
Description
Example Frameworks




Sequential pipeline
Agents execute one after another
LangGraph, CrewAI sequential


Planner-Executor
One agent plans, another executes
Plan-and-Execute in LangChain


Planner-Executor-Critic
Third agent reviews
AutoGen with feedback


Hub-and-Spoke
Central orchestrator, peripheral specialists
HuggingGPT, MetaGPT


Peer debate
Agents argue toward consensus
Society of Mind, debate papers


Hierarchical tree
Manager agents spawn sub-agents
AutoGen with nested chats


Dynamic team
Team composition evolves with task
DyLAN, AgentScope

title: Multi-Agent Systems & Frameworks
text:
Communication Protocols
How agents communicate has evolved significantly:
2022–2023: Natural language Agents communicate via free-form text messages. Flexible but unreliable — agents misunderstand each other.
2023–2024: Structured schemas Agents use JSON or typed outputs. Tools like LangGraph add explicit state types.
2024: Model Context Protocol (MCP) Anthropic released MCP (November 2024) — an open standard for how LLMs connect to tools, data sources, and other agents. Gaining rapid adoption as a universal agent communication standard.

MCP Specification
Supported by: Claude, Cursor, dozens of IDE integrations
Provides: Standardized tool definition, resource access, context management

title: Multi-Agent Systems & Frameworks
text:
Evaluation Benchmarks

AgentBench (2023)
Liu et al. (THU) · arXiv:2308.03688
First comprehensive multi-domain benchmark for agent evaluation. 8 environments: OS, database, knowledge graph, digital card game, lateral thinking puzzle, HouseHolding, web browsing, web shopping.


WebArena (2023)
Zhou et al. · arXiv:2307.13854
Realistic web navigation benchmark with 4 web apps (Reddit, GitLab, e-commerce, maps). 812 tasks. As of 2023, best agents scored ~14%; by 2025, top systems exceed 50%.


GAIA: General AI Assistants Benchmark (2023)
Mialon et al. (Meta AI) · arXiv:2311.12983
Tests “real-world” task completion requiring web search, file reading, coding. Humans score 92%; early GPT-4 agents scored 15%. Designed to require multi-step reasoning with tools.


OSWorld (2024)
Xie et al. · arXiv:2404.07972
Desktop GUI task benchmark (Ubuntu, Windows, macOS). 369 tasks across real applications. As of launch, best agents scored <10%; by early 2025, Claude achieves 72.5%.


SWE-bench (2023)
Jimenez et al. · arXiv:2310.06770
GitHub issue resolution benchmark. 2,294 tasks requiring understanding codebases, editing files, passing tests. Early agents: 2-3%; SWE-agent: 12%; Claude-based systems: 40%+.


WorkArena / WorkArena++ (2024)
Drouin et al. · ServiceNow Research
Enterprise workflow automation benchmark. Tests agents on realistic business software tasks (ServiceNow). WorkArena++: 682 multi-step tasks requiring deep tool use.

Continue to Memory, Tools & Actions →

title: 2024–2026: The Frontier
text:
2024–2025 transformed LLM agents from a research curiosity into a production category. The key shifts:

Coding agents became the killer app — measurable, automated benchmarks drove rapid progress
Major labs shipped agentic products — Computer Use, Operator, Copilot Workspace
Frameworks matured — LangGraph v1.0, AutoGen production, MCP standardization
Safety concerns became urgent — prompt injection, alignment, sandboxing
RL reasoning models (o1, DeepSeek-R1) changed what “agent reasoning” means

title: 2024–2026: The Frontier
text:
2024–2025 transformed LLM agents from a research curiosity into a production category. The key shifts:

Coding agents became the killer app — measurable, automated benchmarks drove rapid progress
Major labs shipped agentic products — Computer Use, Operator, Copilot Workspace
Frameworks matured — LangGraph v1.0, AutoGen production, MCP standardization
Safety concerns became urgent — prompt injection, alignment, sandboxing
RL reasoning models (o1, DeepSeek-R1) changed what “agent reasoning” means

title: 2024–2026: The Frontier
text:
Coding & Software Engineering Agents
The most concrete test of agent capability: can it resolve real GitHub issues?

SWE-bench: The Software Engineering Benchmark (2023)
Jimenez et al. (Princeton) · arXiv:2310.06770
2,294 real GitHub issues from 12 popular Python repos. An agent must read the codebase, understand the bug, edit files, and pass the repo’s test suite. Human developers resolve ~94%; early agents: 2-3%.
SWE-bench Verified (2024): Curated subset of 500 verified tasks. Fairer evaluation.
Progress on SWE-bench:



Model / System
Date
Score




Early GPT-4 baselines
2023
1-3%


SWE-agent + GPT-4
May 2024
12.3%


Devin (Cognition)
Mar 2024
~13%


SWE-agent + Claude 3.5
2024
22.1%


Agentless + Claude
2024
27.3%


Claude 3.5 Sonnet (SWE-bench V)
2025
49.0%


o3 / frontier
Mar 2025
~70%+





SWE-agent (2024)
Yang et al. (Princeton NLP) · arXiv:2405.15793
Dedicated software engineering agent with a custom Agent-Computer Interface (ACI) — carefully designed file editing, navigation, and execution tools optimized for software tasks. ReAct-style reasoning over a repository.

Key insight: Interface design matters as much as the model — the ACI dramatically improves performance vs. raw bash
GitHub: princeton-nlp/SWE-agent · 15k+ stars



Devin: The First AI Software Engineer (March 2024)
Cognition AI · cognition.ai
The product that put AI software engineering on the map. Devin is a proprietary coding agent with a full IDE environment, terminal, and web browser. Claimed ~13% on SWE-bench at launch — remarkable at the time.

Impact: Demonstrated commercial viability of autonomous coding; triggered massive VC investment in the space
Technical details: Proprietary; no published paper



OpenHands (formerly OpenDevin) (2024)
All Hands AI · github.com/All-Hands-AI/OpenHands · 35k+ stars
The open-source answer to Devin. Sandboxed execution, web browser, file system, multiple LLM backends. Active community with enterprise adoption.

Architecture: Containerized sandbox; event-driven agent loop; supports any LLM via LiteLLM



Aider: AI Pair Programming in Your Terminal (2023-2025)
Paul Gauthier · aider.chat · github.com/paul-gauthier/aider
Mature, pragmatic open-source coding assistant. Human-in-the-loop by design — shows diffs before applying, handles git commits. Supports Claude, GPT-4, local models. Among the most widely-used coding tools by developers who want control.

Key differentiator: Transparency and control over full autonomy



CodeAct: Code as the Universal Action Space (2024)
Wang et al. · arXiv:2402.01030
Uses executable Python code as the agent’s action space. Instead of structured JSON tool calls, the agent writes code. More flexible, handles complex multi-step logic, better error recovery.


GitHub Copilot Workspace (2024)
GitHub / Microsoft · Blog
GitHub’s foray into agentic coding: plan → implement → test within GitHub’s ecosystem. Integration with Issues, PRs, and GitHub Actions.

title: 2024–2026: The Frontier
text:
Key 2024–2025 Papers

OSWorld: Benchmarking Multimodal Agents on Desktop Computers (2024)
Xie et al. · arXiv:2404.07972
Real computer tasks across Ubuntu, Windows, macOS with screenshots. 369 tasks in 9 domains. Early systems: <5%; Claude 3.7 Sonnet (early 2025): 72.5%.


GAIA: A Benchmark for General AI Assistants (2023)
Mialon et al. (Meta AI / HuggingFace) · arXiv:2311.12983
Deceptively hard tasks requiring multi-step web search, file analysis, and reasoning. 3 difficulty levels; humans score 92%; early GPT-4 scored 15%. Leaderboard shows rapid progress through 2024-2025.


WorkArena: Workplace Task Automation (2024)
Drouin et al. (ServiceNow) · ServiceNow Research
Enterprise workflow automation in ServiceNow. WorkArena++: 682 compositional tasks requiring understanding of business software. Benchmarks agent capability in realistic enterprise settings.


AgentHarm: Evaluating Agent Safety (2024)
Andriushchenko et al. · arXiv:2410.09024
Benchmark for evaluating harmful agent behaviors. Tests whether agents can be manipulated into violating safety guidelines through indirect channels.


WASP: Wiretapping Agents via Subconscious Persuasion (2024)
Xu et al.
Studies how adversarial prompts embedded in tool outputs (indirect prompt injection) can subvert agent behavior. Important for understanding real-world agent vulnerability.


Tau-bench: Tool-Agent-User Benchmark (2024)
Yao et al. · arXiv:2406.12045
Benchmarks agents on multi-turn, tool-using conversations with simulated users. Evaluates both task completion and user satisfaction. Addresses gap in single-turn evaluation.

title: 2024–2026: The Frontier
text:
Agentic AI Products & Trends

OpenAI
GPTs (November 2023): Custom ChatGPT configurations with tools and knowledge. First mass-market agentic product — millions created.
Assistants API (2023): Developer API for building agents with threads, tools (code interpreter, retrieval, function calling), and file handling.
o1 / o1-mini (September 2024): RL-trained reasoning model. Internal “chain of thought” before answering. Dramatically improves on math, coding, science.
o3 (December 2024 / early 2025): Next-generation reasoning. Solves 25.2% of FrontierMath (vs. 2% for prior models); high ARC-AGI-1 scores.
Operator (January 2025): Browser-using agent for automating web tasks (shopping, forms, reservations). First OpenAI product where the model directly controls a web browser.
Implication for agents: RL reasoning changes what “reasoning” means — it’s now a first-class model capability, not just a prompting trick.


Anthropic
Claude Tool Use (2023): Function calling for Claude — standardized tool integration.
Computer Use (October 2024): Claude 3.5 Sonnet can view screenshots, move mouse, click, type. Marks the shift from “calling APIs” to “using computers.”
Model Context Protocol / MCP (November 2024): Open standard for LLM-tool-data integration. Rapidly adopted across the ecosystem (Cursor, VS Code, dozens of tools). Potentially the USB-C of LLM integrations. - MCP Specification - MCP GitHub
Claude Code (2025): Agentic coding tool in the terminal. Reads and writes files, runs commands, creates PRs. Competes directly with Cursor and Aider.


Google
Project Astra (2024): Research preview of a real-time multimodal assistant that sees and remembers the environment continuously.
Gemini 2.0 (December 2024): Multimodal model with native tool use, code execution, and extended context (2M tokens). First-party agentic features via Google DeepMind.
Vertex AI Agents (2024): Enterprise-grade agent infrastructure on Google Cloud. Grounding, tool calling, evaluation.
NotebookLM (2024): AI research assistant with agent-like document synthesis. Audio overviews feature became widely used.


OpenAI Swarm (October 2024)
github.com/openai/swarm · Experimental
Lightweight, educational multi-agent framework. Two primitives: agents (LLM + instructions + tools) and handoffs (transfer control to another agent). Minimal abstraction, designed for clarity.

title: 2024–2026: The Frontier
text:
Agent Safety & Reliability
Safety became a serious research topic as agents were deployed in production.

Prompt Injection
The core vulnerability: An agent processes data from the environment (web pages, emails, documents). Adversarial content in that data can contain instructions that override the agent’s intended behavior.
Indirect Prompt Injection (2023)
Greshake et al. · arXiv:2302.12173
First systematic study. Demonstrates attacks via web pages, emails, documents. Shows real risk for deployed agents.
InjecAgent: Benchmarking Indirect Injection (2024)
Zhan et al. · arXiv:2403.02691
Benchmark with 1,054 test cases. GPT-4: 24% attack success rate. Shows agents are frequently vulnerable.
AgentDojo (2024)
Debenedetti et al. · arXiv:2406.13352
Framework for evaluating agent security. Tests prompt injection defenses against utility tradeoffs.


Mitigation Approaches

Sandboxing: Isolate agent actions (containerized execution, read-only environments)
Confirmation steps: Require human approval for irreversible actions
Privilege separation: Limit what information agents can access and exfiltrate
Prompt hardening: Instructions that resist injection (partially effective)
Constitutional approaches: Monitor agent behavior against a set of principles



Anthropic: Building Effective Agents (December 2024)
Anthropic · Blog post
Practitioner guide from the frontier lab. Key lessons: - Augmented LLMs > complex frameworks for most tasks - Workflows over agents when the problem is well-defined - Parallelization as a key multi-agent pattern - Simple is better: start with the minimal architecture

Key Practical Resources
Lilian Weng — LLM Powered Autonomous Agents (June 2023)
The most widely linked blog post introduction to the field. Covers planning, memory, tool use with excellent diagrams. Essential reading.
Anthropic — Building Effective Agents (December 2024)
Production lessons from a frontier lab. Covers workflow patterns, multi-agent design, safety.
What We Learned from a Year of Building with LLMs (2024)
Practitioner synthesis from 6 ML engineers. Practical lessons on prompting, evaluation, deployment.
a16z — Emerging Architectures for LLM Applications (June 2023)
VC perspective on the LLM app stack. Introduced now-standard terminology.
Eugene Yan — Patterns for Building LLM Applications (2023)
Systematic survey of engineering patterns: evals, RAG, finetuning, caching, guardrails.

title: 2024–2026: The Frontier
text:
Open Questions & Future Directions

Long-horizon reliability — How do we prevent error accumulation over 100+ step tasks?
Evaluation — How do we evaluate open-ended, creative agent behavior?
Safety at deployment scale — Prompt injection remains unsolved; trust hierarchies unclear
Cost-efficiency — Multi-agent systems with frontier models are expensive; how to scale down?
Verification — How does an agent (or human) verify that a complex task was correctly completed?
Memory consolidation — Episodic → semantic → procedural: still largely manual or hand-coded
Multi-agent coordination — Communication protocol standardization (MCP is a start)
Embodied + digital convergence — Desktop agents + robot agents converging in capability


Back to Taxonomy & Framework → | See Resources → for reading list
